figures.section: Section III. Classical Theories of Money and the National Price Level - Empirical patterns with respect to Marx's theory of money
figures.full_caption: US and UK Gold Prices (Log Scale, 1930 = 100)

variables.name: UK Gold Price
variables.definition: Price of gold in British pounds sterling

variables.name: US Gold Price
variables.definition: Price of gold in US dollars

shaikh_observations: Sharp break in pattern after Britain abandoned the gold standard in 1931
Great Depression, Great Stagflation (1966-1982), and Great Unraveling (1998+) marked by major movements in gold prices
Gold prices rise sharply during periods of economic crisis and instability
context_paragraphs: Figure 5.1 traces the price of gold in the United States and the United Kingdom from the end of the gold standard in 1931, with special attention to its behavior in the Great Depression of the 1930s, the Great Stagnation of the 1960s, and the Great Unraveling of financial bubbles beginning in the 1990s.

In the period from 1966 to 1982 involved a global economic crisis which may be called the Great Stagflation. In its latter phases, in the years from 1976 to 1980, the price of gold rose from $125 to $615 before to subsiding to $375 by 1982.
By the end of 2009, the price of gold had hit $1,173, accompanied by nervous headlines referring to 'continued concerns about the weakening dollar.'
figures.theoretical_significance: Demonstrates gold's role as medium of safety during economic crises and the breakdown of fixed gold price regimes. Shows the dramatic shift from quasi-flexible to fully flexible gold prices after abandonment of gold standard.
key_features: Relatively stable gold prices until 1931 UK abandonment of gold standard
Major price increases during Great Depression, Great Stagflation, and Great Unraveling periods
Logarithmic scale reveals exponential growth in gold prices during crisis periods

figures.section: Section II. The Origins of Modern Money - Essential functions of money
figures.full_caption: UK Wholesale Price Indexes in Pound Sterling, US Dollars, and Ounces of Gold (Log Scale, 1930 = 100)

variables.name: UK WPI in £
variables.definition: UK Wholesale Price Index expressed in pound sterling

variables.name: UK WPI in $
variables.definition: UK Wholesale Price Index expressed in US dollars

variables.name: UK WPI in Gold
variables.definition: UK Wholesale Price Index expressed in ounces of gold (golden price of commodities)

shaikh_observations: All three indexes remain close from 1790 to 1940, and even have roughly the same level in 1940 as they did in 1790
After 1940 the patterns are radically different - the £ and $ prices rise dramatically while gold prices remain relatively stable
The picture looks very different according to the medium in which we choose to express prices
context_paragraphs: Price is the quantitative worth of a commodity expressed through the medium of money. This is a deceptively simple statement, given that money takes so many forms.

Money can function as a medium of pricing in the form of British pounds and a medium of circulation in US dollars. Thus, every commodity has at least two money prices: its national price in local currency (British pounds) and its international price (US dollars).
Our discussion of money as a medium of safety brings out a third possibility: price in terms of gold, which still functions as a universal equivalent.
figures.theoretical_significance: Illustrates Marx's theory that commodity prices can be expressed in multiple monetary forms, and demonstrates the radical shift in price behavior after abandonment of gold as effective medium of pricing around 1939/40.
key_features: Three price measures track closely together 1790-1940
Dramatic divergence post-1940: fiat currency prices rise exponentially while golden prices remain relatively stable
Validates Marx's decomposition of price into golden price of commodities times monetary price of gold

figures.title: Cost Structure in Industry A (Single or Several Co-Equal Conditions of Production)
figures.description: Diagram showing unit costs vs total output for Industry A, depicting a horizontal band representing average unit cost with shaded area around it, and a dotted line indicating the path of future expansion. Represents the case where average conditions of production are the regulating ones.
figures.context: Illustrates regulating conditions of production when there is just one, or several roughly co-equal methods of production (type A1) in use. The dark center line represents the average unit cost, while the shaded band represents distribution of production costs around the average.

figures.title: Cost Structure in Industry B (Agriculture or Mining)
figures.description: Diagram showing unit costs vs total output with three blocks labeled B1, B2, and B3 arranged in ascending order of unit costs. B3 represents the margin of production.
figures.context: Illustrates the case where reproducible conditions of production are the highest cost ones in use. B1 and B2 represent fully utilized conditions, while B3 represents the margin of cultivation—the best reproducible (regulating) condition of production whose unit cost is the foundation for the regulating price of production in agriculture or mining.

figures.title: Cost Structure in Industry C (Older versus Newer Technologies)
figures.description: Diagram showing unit costs vs total output with three blocks labeled C1, C2, and C3 arranged in descending order of unit costs, with dotted lines extending from each block.
figures.context: Illustrates the case where regulating conditions are the lowest cost ones. Higher cost methods C1 and C2 represent older methods which remain in operation but are no longer competitive. C3 embodies the regulating conditions of production.

figures.title: Effect on Profit Rates of Competition within an Industry
figures.description: Three vertical columns labeled Industry A, B, and C, each showing a spectrum of profit rates (rA1, rA2, rA3, etc.) with average rates marked. Shows how profit rates differ within each industry.
figures.context: Depicts the first general effect of real competition: differentiation of profit rates arising from equalization of selling prices due to competition within an industry. Each industry has a spectrum of profit rates arrayed around some particular average rate.

figures.title: Effect on Profit Rates of Competition between Industries
figures.description: Three vertical columns for Industries A, B, and C showing profit rate spectra with starred values (r*A, r*B3, r*C1) indicating regulating rates that are equalized across industries.
figures.context: Shows the second result of real competition: equalization of regulating profit rates across industries. Industry A shows rA = r*A (average conditions are regulating), Industry B shows rB3 = r*B (highest cost conditions are regulating), and Industry C shows rC3 = r*C (lowest cost conditions are regulating).

figures.title: Equalization of Profit Rates over a 'Cycle of Lean and Fat Years'
figures.description: Graph showing crossing profit rate curves labeled r*A and r*B over time, with shaded boxes at crossing points representing the turbulent process of equalization.
figures.context: Illustrates that profit rate equalization is a turbulent process operating over cycles of fat and lean years. Shows that regulating rates will be different in any given year, and it is their 'crossings' rather than equality that characterizes the equalization process.

figures.title: Competition Can Give Rise to Persistent Differences in National Profit Rates
figures.description: Diagram showing three industry columns (A, B, C) divided between Country I (upper portion) and Country II (lower, shaded portion), with starred regulating rates (r*A, r*B3, r*C1) in the overlap region.
figures.context: Demonstrates that if capital location differs by country, competition can lead to persistent differences in national profit rates even while regulating rates are equalized. Shows that for equalization of regulating rates to lead to equalization of national rates, capital within industries would need to be exactly alike.

figures.title: Cost Curves in Andrews and Brunner
figures.description: Graph showing average cost (ac), average variable cost (avc), and average fixed cost (afc) curves plotted against output. Shows normal output point before plant capacity, marking the effective minimum average cost point.
figures.context: Illustrates Andrews and Brunner's view that average cost declines with output because average fixed cost declines while average materials and labor cost tend to be constant. Normal output is set just short of sustainable capacity limit to accommodate desired reserves, defining the effective minimum average cost point.

figures.title: Harrodian Long-Run Equilibrium versus Monopolistic Competition
figures.description: Three panels comparing Perfect Competition, Monopolistic Competition, and Harrodian Competition. Each shows price (p) vs output (X) with p* curve and demand curve (D), illustrating different equilibrium outcomes.
figures.context: Compares three models: (1) Perfect Competition with demand tangent to minimum of p* curve; (2) Monopolistic Competition with demand tangent to p* at higher price; (3) Harrod's model where demand intersects p* at minimum normal price. Shows Harrod's critique that only the minimum normal price is sustainable in long run.

figures.title: Andrews's View on the Empirical Spectrum of Unit Costs with Respect to Plant Scale
figures.description: Scatter plot showing unit cost vs plant scale with X marks indicating observations and a dotted exponential decline curve fitting the pattern. Shows steep falls for small firms with rate of fall progressively slackening.
figures.context: Depicts Andrews's observation that larger firms have lower unit costs, with fairly steep falls in average costs for small firms and progressively slackening rate of fall with size. Compatible with exponential relation k = Ae^(-b·Scale) or log(k) = log(A) - b·Scale.

figures.title: Deviations of US Industry Profit Rates from Average
figures.description: Multi-panel chart showing deviations of profit rates from average for 30 US industries from 1987-2005. Each small panel shows time series for one industry with zero line indicating where rates cross the average.
figures.context: Shows that of 30 industries in the sample, 18 have profit rates that cross the average rate (deviations change sign), while 12 remain persistently above or below. figures.title: Deviations of US Industry Incremental Profit Rates from Average
figures.description: Multi-panel chart showing deviations of incremental profit rates from average for 30 US industries from 1987-2005. All panels show multiple crossings of the zero line.
figures.context: In every single case, individual incremental rates of profit cross back and forth relative to the average incremental rate. Smallest number of crossings is 4 (Fabricated Metals), largest is 12 (Broadcast). Radically different picture from average rates of profit in same sample, providing evidence for equalization of regulating rates of profit.

figures.section: VI. EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE - 5. Profit rate and the interest rate
figures.full_caption: Figure 10.5 Business Profit Rate and Bank Prime Rate
variables: Business Profit Rate (average profit rate of the business sector)
Prime Rate (bank interest rate offered to good business customers)
shaikh_observations: The interest rate is indeed normally lower than the profit rate, as expected at a theoretical level

The rule r > i only applies to the medium term, not to every single year - it was violated for fourteen years during the Great Stagflation of 1967-1982 when rapidly rising prices drove up the interest rate until monetary policy forced it back down
A borrowing rate higher than the average profit rate implies accelerated business failures and plant closings, which would force the interest rate back down by collapsing demand for business loans
context_paragraphs: Figure 10.5 looks at the prime rate of interest, which is the rate offered by banks to their good business customers, in relation to the average profit rate of the business sector (previously derived in appendix 6.7.II and calculated in appendix 6.8A.3).

We see that the interest rate is indeed normally lower than the profit rate, as expected at a theoretical level. Given that the average profit rate is an amalgam of strong and weak firms and new and older plants, a borrowing rate higher than the average profit rate implies accelerated business failures and plant closings.

The rule r > i only applies to the medium term, not to every single year. Indeed, it is in fact violated for fourteen years in the latter part of the Great Stagflation of 1967-1982 when a rapidly rising price level drove up the interest rate until monetary policy forced the latter to fall thereafter.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure demonstrates the classical proposition that the interest rate must be less than the profit rate for capitalist accumulation to proceed (equation 10.1). The extended violation during the Great Stagflation period (when i > r) helps explain the economic difficulties of that era and validates the classical theory's prediction that such a condition cannot be sustained indefinitely. The figure also illustrates the connection between interest rates, the price level, and monetary policy intervention.
key_features: Clear identification of the Great Stagflation period (1967-1982) marked on the figure
Shows the business profit rate falling from around 16% in the early period to below 6% by the late period
Prime rate peaks at around 18-20% during the early 1980s Volcker disinflation
Extended period (14 years) where i > r during the stagflation era, demonstrating this is economically unsustainable
Return to normal pattern (r > i) after monetary policy intervention in early 1980s

shaikh_observations: The far greater volatility of the stock market rate of return makes both the average profit rate and Shiller's constant discount rate seem stable by comparison

The close correspondence between the equity return and the incremental rate of profit in figure 10.11 versus the lack of correspondence in figure 10.12 makes it clear that the problem lies in the specific hypothesis about the regulator of the stock market

If we compare the volatile actual stock market rate to any constant such as Shiller's discount rate (7.6%) or even to the time-varying average rate of profit, the stock market rate always exhibits considerable 'excess volatility'
context_paragraphs: The underlying difficulty becomes immediately evident if we turn the problem around. Shiller himself says that the 'efficient markets assumption [is] that expected returns on the market are constant through time, that there are no good or bad times to enter the stock market in terms of predictable returns.'

This is exactly the problem, for if we compare the volatile actual stock market rate to any constant such as Shiller's most recent discount rate 7.6% or even to the time-varying average rate of profit, the stock market rate always exhibits considerable 'excess volatility.'

The close correspondence between the equity return and the incremental rate of profit in figure 10.11 and the lack of correspondence between the former and the constant discount rate or even the average rate of profit in figure 10.12 makes it clear that the problem lies in the specific hypothesis about the regulator of the stock market.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure demonstrates that Shiller's 'excess volatility puzzle' is an artifact of comparing the actual stock market rate to the wrong benchmark (either a constant discount rate or the average profit rate). The classical theory proposes that the equity rate of return is regulated by the INCREMENTAL (not average) rate of profit, which explains why this figure shows apparent excess volatility while figure 10.11 shows close correspondence. This is a fundamental critique of the Efficient Market Hypothesis and dividend-discount models.

Corporate average rate of profit falls from over 16% to below 6% over the period

Visual comparison clearly shows that comparing actual equity returns to either benchmark makes the stock market appear excessively volatile
The figure spans 64 years (1947-2011), covering multiple business cycles and market regimes

figures.section: V. Real Competition Implies Absolute Cost Advantage - Section 1: Introduction
figures.full_caption: Figure 11.1 The Ricardian Duality

variables.name: Comparative Price - Wine
variables.description: Portuguese wine price relative to English wine price

variables.name: Comparative Price - Cloth
variables.description: Portuguese cloth price relative to English cloth price

variables.name: International Regulating Price Ratio
variables.description: International relative price of cloth to wine
variables.behavior: Remains constant outside Ricardian range (1.11-1.50 £/€), only varies within that range

variables.name: Ricardian Comparative Costs Region
variables.description: Exchange rate range (1.11-1.50 £/€) where comparative cost theory applies

shaikh_observations: The figure reveals a fundamental duality in Ricardo's theory: comparative commodity prices change smoothly with the exchange rate, but international ruling prices remain cost-bound except within the narrow Ricardian range (1.11-1.50 £/€).

For exchange rates below 1.11 £/€, Portugal dominates both industries (both comparative prices below 1); within the Ricardian range England dominates cloth while Portugal retains wine; above 1.50 £/€ England's capitals rule both industries.

This duality arises from an inconsistency between Ricardo's exposition and his prior classical foundations - correction of this error removes the duality and invalidates Ricardo's claim that free trade transforms absolute cost advantages into comparative ones.
context_paragraphs: In the first chart we see that the comparative prices of Portuguese to English producers changes smoothly with the exchange rate, and that these are both less than one for exchange rates below 1.11 £/€ so that Portugal dominates both international industries; that in the exchange rate range 1.11 – 1.50 £/€ the Portuguese comparative price for cloth is above one (hence England's below one) while that for wine is still below one, so England now dominates cloth production while Portugal retains wine; and for exchange rates greater than 1.50 £/€, Portuguese comparative prices are both above one so that England's capitals rule both industries.

In the second chart, we see that international relative prices nonetheless do not change at all when we are outside the comparative cost range. We will see that this duality arises from an inconsistency between Ricardo's exposition and his prior classical foundations. Correction of this error removes the duality. But then Ricardo's claim that free trade transforms absolute cost advantages into comparative ones is also invalidated.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure is central to Shaikh's critique of Ricardian comparative advantage theory. It demonstrates that Ricardo's theory only works within a narrow exchange rate range (the 'comparative costs region'), and outside this range, absolute cost advantages determine trade patterns. This undermines the standard neoclassical claim that comparative advantage always prevails under free trade. The duality shown - smooth changes in comparative prices vs. flat international price ratios outside the Ricardian range - exposes a fundamental inconsistency in Ricardo's logic that has been overlooked in orthodox trade theory.
key_features: Two-panel conceptual diagram showing the Ricardian duality
Three distinct regions: Portugal dominates (e < 1.11), comparative costs region (1.11 < e < 1.50), UK dominates (e > 1.50)
Demonstrates that comparative advantage only applies in narrow exchange rate range
Shows discontinuity between smooth comparative price changes and constant international price ratios
Critical for understanding Shaikh's argument that absolute cost advantages, not comparative advantages, govern international competition
figures.data_notes: This is a conceptual/theoretical figure derived from the numerical example in Tables 11.1-11.3 (pages 18-19) using Portuguese cloth (£100/€90 = 1.11) and wine (£120/€80 = 1.5) comparative price ratios as the boundaries of the Ricardian range.

variables.name: Real Effective Exchange Rate (US)
variables.description: US real effective exchange rate relative to major trading partners, based on producer price indexes
variables.behavior: Shows turbulent gravitation around adjusted real effective unit labor costs

variables.name: Adjusted Real Effective Unit Labor Costs (US)
variables.description: US manufacturing real direct unit labor costs relative to trading partners, adjusted for nontradable/tradable goods effects using CPI/PPI ratio
variables.measurement: Index rescaled to match period average of real exchange rate
variables.source: BLS data on unit labor costs and CPI
variables.proxy_note: Manufacturing real direct unit labor costs used as proxy for vertically integrated real unit labor costs due to lack of input-output data

shaikh_observations: The US real effective exchange rate gravitates around the corresponding adjusted real unit labor costs over the 1960-2008 period, confirming the classical hypothesis that real exchange rates are regulated by relative competitive positions measured through real unit labor costs.

Sharp deviations from fundamentals occur in 1980-1987 (linked to large interest rate differential leading to capital inflows) and 1997-2003 (associated with equity price bubble), but the exchange rate returns toward its cost-determined center of gravity.

The long-run stability of this relationship provides a robust policy rule-of-thumb for sustainable real exchange rates, superior to the widely used PPP hypothesis which fails empirically (recall Figure 11.3).
context_paragraphs: Figures 11.4 and 11.5 show that the real effective exchange rates of the United States and Japan do indeed gravitate around the corresponding real unit labor costs (adjusted for nontradable/tradable effects), both variables being defined relative to the trading partners of the country in question. Given that the price data involves index numbers whose scale is arbitrarily defined by the base year (2002 = 100), the real unit labor cost variable was rescaled to have the same period average as the real exchange rate. This facilitates visual comparison but, of course, has no effect on the econometric tests.

In the United States, the real exchange rate deviates sharply from its fundamentals in the 1980–1987 and 1997–2003 periods but then returns toward it. The first period has been widely discussed in the literature, and there is considerable debate over its underlying causes. One prominent explanation has been that the large run-up in the interest rate differential between the United States and its trading partners led to large short-term capital inflows which in turn gradually extinguished the interest rate differential (Friedman 1991). The second period is associated with the equity price bubble from the late 1990s to the early 2000s.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure provides crucial empirical validation of Shaikh's classical theory of exchange rates based on absolute cost advantages (real unit labor costs) rather than the neoclassical purchasing power parity (PPP) or comparative advantage theories. The turbulent gravitation pattern - where exchange rates deviate but return to their cost-determined center of gravity - is characteristic of classical competition theory. This validates equation (11.16) which states that the real exchange rate er ≡ (pe/p*) is regulated by the ratio of regulating unit labor costs adjusted for nontradable/tradable goods effects.
key_features: Time series data covering 48 years (1960-2008)
Two series: Real Effective Exchange Rate (actual market outcome) and Adjusted Real Effective Unit Labor Costs (theoretical center of gravity)
Demonstrates turbulent gravitation - exchange rate oscillates around but returns to its cost-determined fundamentals
Notable deviations in 1980-1987 (interest rate differential) and 1997-2003 (equity bubble)
Empirical confirmation of classical theory over neoclassical alternatives
Index rescaled for visual comparison (same period average)
figures.data_notes: Producer price indexes used for real exchange rate construction; BLS data on manufacturing unit labor costs and CPI used for real unit labor costs; CPI/PPI ratio used as proxy for nontradable/tradable price ratio. All variables measured relative to trade-weighted average of major trading partners. ARDL econometric method used to test long-run relationship (results in Tables 11.5-11.6, page 44). Real interest rate differential found statistically significant for US short-run deviations.

shaikh_observations: The Japanese real effective exchange rate gravitates around the corresponding adjusted real unit labor costs over the 1960-2008 period, confirming that even with multiple exchange rate interventions, the real exchange rate is anchored by competitive fundamentals (real unit labor costs).

Despite well-known exchange rate interventions in 1976-1978, 1985-1988 (Plaza Accord), 1992-1996, and 1998-2004, the exchange rate maintains a long-run relationship with real unit labor costs, though the real interest rate differential was not statistically significant for Japan (unlike the US).

The visible correspondence between the two series, even given data limitations and empirical approximations (using manufacturing direct unit labor costs as proxy for integrated costs, CPI/PPI for tradable/nontradable ratio), demonstrates the robustness of the classical cost-based theory of exchange rates.
context_paragraphs: Figures 11.4 and 11.5 show that the real effective exchange rates of the United States and Japan do indeed gravitate around the corresponding real unit labor costs (adjusted for nontradable/tradable effects), both variables being defined relative to the trading partners of the country in question. Given that the price data involves index numbers whose scale is arbitrarily defined by the base year (2002 = 100), the real unit labor cost variable was rescaled to have the same period average as the real exchange rate. This facilitates visual comparison but, of course, has no effect on the econometric tests.

In the case of Japan, the matter is complicated by several well-known short-term interventions in the exchange rate market. The most significant of these are deemed to have been in 1976–1978, 1985–1988 (Plaza Accord), 1992–1996, and 1998–2004 (Nanto 2007, CRS-4). In this light, we test whether interest rate differentials remain influential in explaining the deviations of the Japanese real exchange rate from its fundamentals.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure provides parallel empirical validation to Figure 11.4 for Japan, demonstrating that Shaikh's classical cost-based theory of exchange rates holds even in cases with known government interventions. The persistence of the gravitational relationship despite Plaza Accord and other interventions shows that market forces driven by competitive cost structures ultimately dominate policy interventions. This is particularly significant because Japan's case is often cited as evidence of successful exchange rate management, yet the figure shows that even these interventions could not break the fundamental link between exchange rates and relative unit labor costs.

figures.section: I. INTRODUCTION - Section 6. Accounting for aggregate demand, supply, and capacity - Subsection vii. Normal capacity utilization does not imply Say's Law
figures.full_caption: Output, Costs, and Normal Capacity
variables: YRn (Normal capacity real output at lowest cost point)
ac' (average cost with higher variable costs)
shaikh_observations: Normal capacity output (YRn = 20) is defined by the lowest cost point of production, which in this example occurs at the end of the first shift, well below engineering capacity (YRmax = 45 = 220% of normal)

Normal capacity utilization can be well below the engineering rate, indicating that firms typically have substantial unused capacity, but this does not indicate excess capacity because an optimal amount exists based on economic costs

Changes in variable costs (illustrated by ac' curve) need not affect the normal rate of capacity utilization because the minimum cost point remains at the same output level (YRn = 20) even when costs shift
context_paragraphs: To illustrate these notions, consider an average plant with engineering capacity YRmax = 45, which can be potentially operated up to two equal shifts, each shift producing up to 20 units of output. Suppose we adopt the common assumption that unit prime costs (average variable costs avc) are constant at all levels of output within a given shift. The first shift has overhead costs = 100 and unit prime costs = 20, while the second shift has a 30% premium on both of these items.

The resulting average fixed cost (afc), average variable cost (avc), and average cost (ac) curves are depicted in figure 12.1 as solid lines. Under the assumed cost structures, the lowest average cost point is at the end of the first shift which defines long-run economic capacity, that is, the normal level of output (YRn = 20), though, of course, under different cost curves it could equally well be at the end of the second or even third shift.

The fact that the normal rate of capacity utilization can be well below the engineering rate tells us that firms typically have a great deal of short-term unused capacity. Capital is never fully occupied. But in itself this is not evidence of persistent excess capacity because 'an optimal amount of [unused capacity] exists and depends on economic costs'.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure is central to Shaikh's argument against neoclassical full employment theory and post-Keynesian free-variable capacity utilization. It demonstrates that normal capacity is determined by cost-competitive considerations (the lowest cost point) rather than engineering limits or demand levels. The distinction between YRn (normal capacity at minimum cost), actual output YR, and YRmax (engineering capacity) is crucial for understanding: (1) why capital is 'never fully occupied' in the Ricardian sense that Marx criticized; (2) why normal capacity utilization (uKn = 100%) can persist even with substantial unused engineering capacity; and (3) why the normal rate can be largely immune to variations in actual utilization rates. This framework allows Shaikh to incorporate Harrod's investment accelerator (firms invest when actual exceeds normal utilization) while rejecting both the neoclassical Say's Law (supply creates its own demand at full employment) and the post-Keynesian notion that capacity utilization is a permanently free variable.
key_features: Displays three cost curves: average fixed cost (afc, declining hyperbola), average variable cost (avc, horizontal then stepped), and average total cost (ac, U-shaped)
Shows two distinct shifts with different cost structures (second shift has 30% premium)
Illustrates that minimum average cost point (YRn = 20) occurs at end of first shift
Demonstrates large gap between normal capacity (YRn = 20) and engineering capacity (YRmax = 45)
Includes dotted line (ac') showing that cost shifts need not change the normal utilization rate
Visual representation of the argument that normal capacity utilization is cost-competitive, not demand-determined

figures.section: II. PRE-KEYNESIAN MACROECONOMICS - Section 3. Pre-Keynesian neoclassical orthodoxy - Subsection ii. The neoclassical argument on full employment supply
figures.full_caption: The Neoclassical Full Employment Outcome
variables: Ld(w/p) (Labor demand, decreasing in real wage)
Ls(w/p) (Labor supply, increasing in real wage)
shaikh_observations: The neoclassical argument posits that labor market equilibrium at (w/p)* and L* determines a unique full employment level, which then determines output YR* from the production function—this is the basis for Say's Law in its neoclassical form

Any attempt by unions or the state to raise real wages above (w/p)* would create unemployment because labor demand would fall below labor supply, thereby proving that market-determined wages are optimal

The construction assumes diminishing returns (declining mpl) which makes labor demand a negative function of the real wage, and assumes substitution effects dominate income effects so that labor supply is a positive function of the real wage
context_paragraphs: Under the assumed conditions of perfect competition, the ith individual firm maximizes its profit by producing an output YRi such that its marginal costs equal the ruling market price: mci ≡ w/mpli = p, which implies w/p = mpli. Skipping blithely over all aggregation issues, this condition is then transformed into an equivalent aggregate condition (w/p) = mpl.

The individual firm's production function is similarly scaled up to yield a short-run aggregate production function YR = A · F(KR, L) in which real output (YR) is the maximum real output that can be produced from a given real capital stock KR and some amount of labor (L). With a given capital stock and given level of technology (represented by the parameter A), short-run aggregate output is solely a function of labor input.

Equilibrium in the labor market then determines a particular market-clearing real wage and corresponding level of full employment (which subsumes frictional unemployment due to the time of transition between jobs). The full employment of the labor supply requires a corresponding level of output from the production function, as shown in figure 12.2: beginning in the lower panel from the labor market equilibrium at real wage (w/p)* and employment L*, one moves up from the latter to the production function in the top panel and then across to the corresponding full employment output (YR*).
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure encapsulates the pre-Keynesian neoclassical orthodoxy that Keynes attacked. It represents the theoretical foundation for: (1) Say's Law—that supply creates its own demand because output is determined by labor supply and the production function, not by aggregate demand; (2) the claim that unemployment can only be voluntary or due to wage rigidities; (3) the proposition that government intervention is neither necessary nor desirable since markets automatically clear at full employment. Shaikh uses this as a foil for both Keynesian demand-side arguments and for his own classical approach. The diagram's internal logic depends critically on: the validity of aggregate production functions (which Shaikh rejects in Chapter 3), the assumption of diminishing returns, the aggregation from individual to aggregate labor demand/supply (which ignores heterogeneity), and perfect competition (which Shaikh argues is internally inconsistent). The figure also illustrates why neoclassicals could not explain the Great Depression without invoking wage rigidities.
key_features: Two-panel diagram linking labor market equilibrium (bottom) to output determination (top)
Production function YR = A · f(KR, L) with diminishing returns (concave shape)
Downward-sloping labor demand curve Ld derived from w/p = mpl condition
Upward-sloping labor supply curve Ls based on substitution effect dominating income effect
Unique equilibrium point (L*, (w/p)*) determining full employment
Horizontal and vertical dotted lines connecting labor market equilibrium to production function outcome

figures.section: II. PRE-KEYNESIAN MACROECONOMICS - Section 3. Pre-Keynesian neoclassical orthodoxy - Subsection iii. The neoclassical argument on aggregate demand and the interest rate
figures.full_caption: The Neoclassical Savings–Investment Adjustment
variables: I (Investment, decreasing function of interest rate i)
S (Savings, increasing function of interest rate i)
ir (Real interest rate, determined by real profit rate in long run)
shaikh_observations: The neoclassical argument claims that the interest rate adjusts to equate savings and investment, thereby ensuring that aggregate demand (C + I) automatically equals aggregate supply (C + S = Y) at the full employment output level—this is the mechanism underlying Say's Law

If households decide to save less (consume more), the savings curve shifts inward, raising the interest rate and reducing investment until ED returns to zero; since aggregate supply remains at full employment throughout, increased consumption is exactly offset by decreased investment—demonstrating that demand management is futile

This framework assumes investment is the demand for loanable funds and savings is the supply, with the interest rate as the price that clears the market—Keynes's liquidity preference theory directly challenges this by making the interest rate dependent on money demand and supply rather than savings and investment
context_paragraphs: Full employment output is only sustainable if it can be sold, which requires a sufficient amount of aggregate demand. To understand the logic of the basic neoclassical model, it is useful to consider the expression for aggregate excess demand ED = [I–S] in equation (12.6). Neoclassical theory assumes that private investment is a negative function, and private savings is a positive function, of the real interest rate (ir). Private investment is simultaneously viewed as the demand for loanable funds and savings as the supply as depicted in figure 12.3.

Then excess demand in the commodity market, ED > 0, also implies an excess demand for loanable funds in the funds market. The latter would drive up the interest rate, decreasing investment and increasing savings until ED = 0—in which case aggregate demand is equal to aggregate supply. Since aggregate supply is fixed at the full employment level, aggregate demand does all the adjusting through the effects of the real interest rate. This is a version of Say's Law.

If households were to decide to save less (i.e., consume more), their savings curve will shift inward which will raise the interest rate and hence reduce investment until excess demand is back to zero. But since aggregate commodity supply remains at the full employment level throughout, this can only mean that the increased consumption demand of households has been exactly offset by the lowered investment demand of firms.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure represents the neoclassical loanable funds theory that automatically equilibrates savings and investment through interest rate adjustments, thereby completing the pre-Keynesian argument for Say's Law. It is the second pillar of the neoclassical full employment equilibrium (the first being Fig12.2's labor market clearing). Keynes's General Theory directly attacked this mechanism by arguing that: (1) the interest rate is determined by liquidity preference (money demand/supply) not loanable funds; (2) investment depends on expected profits and 'animal spirits' which are volatile and not reliably responsive to interest rates; (3) savings adjusts to investment through income changes (the multiplier), not through interest rate changes. Shaikh uses this figure to set up the contrast between neoclassical supply-determined output (with demand passively adjusting via interest rates) and Keynesian demand-determined output (with savings passively adjusting via the multiplier). The figure also illustrates why neoclassicals believed government intervention was unnecessary and counterproductive—any fiscal stimulus would just 'crowd out' private investment through higher interest rates.
key_features: Downward-sloping investment curve I(i) reflecting negative relationship between investment and interest rate
Upward-sloping savings curve S(i) reflecting positive relationship between savings and interest rate
Intersection point at i* where ED = 0 and S* = I*
Horizontal axis measuring both savings and investment in same units
Implicit assumption that both curves are stable functions (challenged by Keynes)
Visual representation of loanable funds theory where interest rate is 'price' clearing the market

figures.section: III. KEYNES'S BREAKTHROUGH - Section 3. The Hicksian IS–LM representation of Keynesian economics
figures.full_caption: Hicks's IS–LM Summary of Keynes's Core Arguments
variables: S (Savings, function of income through consumption)
Md (Money demand, function of Y and i)
re (Expected rate of profit on new capital)
shaikh_observations: The IS–LM framework shows that equilibrium output Y* is simultaneously determined by the investment-savings balance (IS curve) and the money demand-supply balance (LM curve), and this equilibrium need not occur at full employment (Y* ≠ YFE), contradicting neoclassical theory

Expansionary fiscal policy shifts IS curve upward, raising equilibrium output but also raising interest rate; expansionary monetary policy shifts LM curve outward, raising output while lowering interest rate; appropriate combination can achieve full employment without changing interest rates

Hicks's formulation eliminates Keynes's emphasis on volatile expectations by making investment a stable negative function of the interest rate and money demand a stable function of current (not expected) income and interest rate—this 'domestication' of Keynes prepared the ground for the neoclassical counterrevolution
context_paragraphs: Leaving aside autonomous consumption, the Keynesian multiplier relation in (12.12) reduces to Y* = I(re–i)/s, where both Y and I are in nominal terms and the volatility of the expected rate of profit plays the dominant central role. Hicks eliminates the expected profit rate altogether by citing Keynes own argument that investment proceeds to the point where profit rate on the last investment put in place is equal to the interest rate. Then investment, and hence output for any given savings rate, becomes a simple negative function of the interest rate.

Hicks's LM relation, which is also in nominal terms, similarly downplays the important role of expectations in determining the possibly volatile holdings of money for precautionary and speculative purposes. He reduces the demand for money to a stable positive function of the level of current (rather than expected) income and a negative function of the interest rate (since a higher interest rate of financial assets will induce agents to hold less idle money balances).

The intersection of the IS and LM curves, as depicted in figure 12.4, would simultaneously determine the equilibrium levels of output and interest rate. The key point from a Keynesian perspective is that the equilibrium output Y* could be different from full employment output YFE.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure represents Hicks's influential 1937 formalization of Keynes's General Theory, which became the dominant framework for macroeconomic analysis for several decades. It is significant because: (1) it demonstrates that equilibrium can occur with unemployment (Y* < YFE), contradicting neoclassical full employment theory; (2) it shows how fiscal and monetary policy can shift the curves to achieve full employment; (3) it integrates the real sector (IS) with the monetary sector (LM) in a coherent framework. However, Shaikh notes critical problems: Hicks eliminated Keynes's emphasis on volatile expectations driven by fundamental uncertainty, replacing them with stable functional relationships; this made the IS-LM framework compatible with the neoclassical framework and prepared the ground for Friedman's monetarist counterattack and Lucas's rational expectations revolution. The figure also illustrates the Keynesian rejection of both Say's Law (since Y* ≠ YFE) and the classical dichotomy (since money affects real output). Modern post-Keynesians criticize the IS-LM framework for treating expectations as stable, but it remains central to intermediate macroeconomics textbooks.
key_features: Downward-sloping IS curve representing combinations of Y and i where investment equals savings (I = S)
Upward-sloping LM curve representing combinations of Y and i where money demand equals money supply (Md = Ms)
Intersection at (Y*, i*) determining simultaneous equilibrium in both commodity and money markets
Vertical line at YFE showing full employment output level to the right of Y*
Gap between Y* and YFE illustrating Keynesian unemployment equilibrium
Implicit representation of fiscal policy (shifts IS) and monetary policy (shifts LM)

figures.section: IV. THE RETURN OF NEO-WALRASIAN ECONOMICS - Section 2. The natural rate of unemployment and inflation in the context of adaptive expectations - Subsection v. The link to inflation
figures.full_caption: Short- and Long-Run Expectations-Augmented Phillips

y_axis.label: Ẇ/W (Rate of Change of Nominal Wages)
key_points: Points A, B, C, D, E marking adjustment path

variables: Ẇ/W (Rate of change of nominal wages)
Short-run Phillips curves (shift with expected inflation)
Long-run Phillips curve (vertical line at uL*)
shaikh_observations: The expectations-augmented Phillips curve framework shows that any attempt to maintain unemployment below the natural rate uL* requires continuously accelerating inflation, as the economy moves from A→C→D and the short-run Phillips curve shifts upward with rising expected inflation—this became the theoretical foundation for the NAIRU (Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment)

In Friedman and Phelps's adaptive expectations version, an unexpected increase in aggregate demand initially moves the economy from A to C along the original Phillips curve, but as expectations adapt, the curve shifts upward and unemployment gradually returns to uL* at point E—the adjustment takes time because expectations are backward-looking

In Lucas's rational expectations version, agents immediately recognize the true inflation rate, so the economy jumps directly from C to E without the gradual adjustment through point D—this eliminates the extended real effects of monetary policy and provides the foundation for the policy ineffectiveness proposition
context_paragraphs: In a static economy with a constant price level, the initial equilibrium position is at the natural rate of unemployment at a corresponding constant money wage, that is, a zero rate of change of nominal wages, which is point A on the initial Phillips curve. In Friedman's argument, an acceleration in the money supply would tend to raise all prices in all markets. If firms were to correctly perceive that all prices had been raised proportionally, they would know that their relative prices were unchanged and would therefore leave their supply unchanged. If workers were to be similarly perceptive, they would know that their nominal wages would now have to grow at the same rate as this higher objective rate of inflation, in which case the Phillips curve would jump to point E.

But Friedman assumes that firms tend to misperceive a rise in their nominal product price as being a partial rise in their relative price due to a local increase in demand, so they raise output and employment to some degree and unemployment falls to point B on the diagram. On the other side, workers initially mistakenly expect prices to continue to be constant so the original Phillips curve remains in force. Then a drop in the unemployment rate provokes a positive growth rate in nominal wages which moves the economy to point C.

However, as firms catch on to the fact that the increase in their costs (input price and nominal wage increases) are greater than originally expected, they reduce output and employment and the unemployment rate starts to move back toward the natural rate. Meanwhile, as workers catch on to the reality of higher prices, their expected rate of inflation rises so that the Phillips curve shifts upward. The net result is a move to point D. This process continues until both sets of perceptions fully catch up to the reality of higher inflation—that is, until the Phillips curve has shifted to the extent that it intersects the vertical line passing through points A and E.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure is pivotal in the history of macroeconomic thought because it represents the theoretical counterrevolution against Keynesian demand management. It synthesizes Friedman's (1968) and Phelps's (1967, 1968) critiques of the original Phillips curve into a framework that: (1) denies the existence of a permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment; (2) establishes that attempts to maintain unemployment below uL* lead to accelerating inflation (the foundation for NAIRU); (3) demonstrates that only unexpected policy changes have real effects, and even these are temporary; (4) implies that systematic monetary policy is ineffective (only 'surprises matter'). The vertical long-run Phillips curve became the cornerstone of monetarist and New Classical macroeconomics, decisively shifting the profession away from Keynesian activism. Shaikh uses this figure to set up his alternative classical analysis in Chapter 14, where he argues that there is indeed a normal rate of unemployment generated by competition itself (as in Marx-Goodwin), but that deviations from this rate need not generate accelerating inflation—the latter requires a separate theory of inflation (Chapter 15).
key_features: Multiple short-run Phillips curves that shift upward with rising expected inflation
Vertical long-run Phillips curve at natural rate uL*
Labeled points A, B, C, D, E tracing adjustment path from low inflation equilibrium to high inflation equilibrium
Point A: Initial equilibrium at uL* with zero inflation and zero expected inflation
Point C: Short-run outcome with lower unemployment but higher wage inflation
Point E: New long-run equilibrium at uL* with higher inflation equal to expected inflation
Arrows indicating direction of adjustment as expectations adapt
Visual demonstration that short-run gain (A to C) is temporary and undone in long run (C to E)

figures.section: III. Dynamical Interactions Between the Wage Share, Unemployment Rate, and the Harrodian 'Natural Rate' of Growth
figures.subsection: 1. Theory of the real wage: From stochastic micro to macro
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.1 Average Real Wage per Worker

axis_specifications.axis_labels: Real Wage on vertical axis, Real Profit per Worker on horizontal axis

shaikh_observations: The real value added per worker (yrt) sets the abstract upper limit to the real wage, while a historically determined minimum real wage (wrmint) sets the lower limit
The socially achievable product-wage (wr*) lies between upper and lower limits, determined by the conflict between labor and capital
Disparate individual capital-labor struggles in a particular social climate lead to a particular ratio (βt) between the average real wage and productivity
context_paragraphs: The classical economists always understood that the real wage had a social and historical component. The history of labor struggles around wages and working conditions certainly bears this out. The conflict between labor and capital brings about a particular division of the money value-added received by the firm.

The abstract upper limit to the real wage is the real value added per worker, that is, the productivity of labor (yrt). The socially achievable product-wage (wr*t), the real wage measured in terms of the price of the product, lies between upper and lower limits.

We can arrive at a relation between the achievable real wage and productivity from the microeconomic level in the manner previously developed in chapter 3, section III, during the derivation of market laws such as downward sloping demand curves.
figures.theoretical_significance: This diagram establishes the theoretical foundation for wage determination in classical theory, showing that wages are not market-clearing variables but outcomes of social struggle constrained by productivity limits. The wage-profit trade-off depicted here is fundamental to understanding how labor strength affects distribution.
key_features: Downward-sloping wage-profit trade-off line from yr to origin
Feasible range marked between wrmin and yr on vertical axis
Point A marking the average normal real wage and profit per worker
Demonstrates that productivity sets maximum wage while historical factors set minimum

figures.section: III. Dynamical Interactions Between the Wage Share, Unemployment Rate, and the Harrodian 'Natural Rate' of Growth
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.2 The Classical Curve

axis_specifications.y_axis: Rate of change of wage share (σW/σW)
axis_specifications.axis_labels: Unemployment rate on horizontal axis, rate of change of wage share on vertical axis
axis_specifications.critical_points: uL* (critical unemployment rate where wage share is stable), uLFE (full employment rate)

variables: Rate of change of wage share (σ˙W/σW)
shaikh_observations: The Classical curve implies a stable wage share at an unemployment rate u*L that will generally differ from the effective full employment rate uLFE, demonstrating that involuntary unemployment is normal under capitalism

A downward shift in this curve implies a reduction in the 'reactive strength' of labor, since any given level of unemployment would then elicit a slower rate of growth of real wages relative to productivity

The level of the wage share and the reactiveness of labor are two distinct dimensions of labor strength - movements along the curve represent changes in actual conditions while shifts represent changes in institutional power
context_paragraphs: If we define the wage share as σW = wr/yr, then equation (14.6) implies that the rate of change of the wage share is a negative function of the unemployment rate. This Classical curve is depicted in figure 14.2.

Such a curve appears as one of the two dynamic relations in Goodwin's elegant formalization of Marx's theory of the Reserve Army of Labor (RAL). As discussed in section II, Goodwin's own derivation is predicated on the assumption of a real wage Phillips curve coupled with a constant rate of growth of productivity.
The Classical curve in figure 14.2 implies a stable wage share at an unemployment rate u*L that will generally differ from the effective full employment rate uLFE.
figures.theoretical_significance: The Classical curve is central to understanding how capitalism creates and maintains involuntary unemployment. Unlike neoclassical theory where unemployment is voluntary, or Keynesian theory where it can be eliminated through demand management, the Classical curve shows that competition itself generates a normal rate of involuntary unemployment. This is the formal expression of Marx's Reserve Army of Labor.
key_features: Downward-sloping curve showing negative relationship between unemployment and wage share growth
Crosses horizontal axis at u*L (critical unemployment rate)
u*L is generally greater than uLFE, indicating persistent involuntary unemployment
Curve can shift down if labor strength is weakened institutionally
Implies wage share rises when uL < u*L and falls when uL > u*L

figures.section: III. Dynamical Interactions Between the Wage Share, Unemployment Rate, and the Harrodian 'Natural Rate' of Growth
figures.subsection: 5. Effects of productivity and labor force growth
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.3 Effects of a Temporary Demand Boost on Wage Share, Unemployment, and Normal Capacity Profit Rate

axis_specifications.y_axis_right: Profit Rate and Unemployment Rate (6% to 14%)

shaikh_observations: A temporary acceleration in aggregate demand initially raises the wage share, reduces the normal capacity profit rate (though realized profit rate may rise for a while due to excess demand) and reduces the unemployment rate, but in the end all three variables return to their normal values
The temporary boost permanently alters the levels of output and employment paths even though growth rates return to normal
The adjustment process shows the classical feedback mechanism: lower unemployment raises wages, which lowers profits, which eventually restores unemployment to its normal rate
context_paragraphs: Figure 14.3 shows that a temporary acceleration in aggregate demand initially raises the wage share, reduces the normal capacity profit rate (though the realized profit rate may rise for a while due to excess demand) and reduces the unemployment rate. But in the end, all three variables return to their normal values.

The patterns in the case of a sustained increase in exogenous demand are quite different. Now we see in figure 14.6 that the wage share remains at a sustained higher level and the profit rate falls correspondingly.
These events highlight the crucial issue of the speed of adjustment along the curve, which is addressed along with other empirical evidence in section VI.
figures.theoretical_significance: This simulation demonstrates the classical proposition that temporary demand boosts have only temporary effects on distribution and unemployment, validating Marx's Reserve Army mechanism. It shows that even strong demand stimulus cannot permanently eliminate unemployment without changing the underlying power relations between labor and capital.
key_features: Wage share rises from ~60% to ~62% then returns to ~60%
Profit rate falls from ~12% to ~11% then returns to ~12%
Unemployment rate falls from ~10% to ~9% then returns to ~10%
All three variables show clear cyclical pattern returning to baseline
Demonstrates temporary nature of demand-driven changes in class struggle variables
figures.appendix_reference: Appendix 14.1 for model parameters and simulation details
related_equations: gYR = fYR[(1 - σW) · Rn - in] + ε (14.10)

figures.section: III. Dynamical Interactions Between the Wage Share, Unemployment Rate, and the Harrodian 'Natural Rate' of Growth
figures.subsection: 5. Effects of productivity and labor force growth
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.4 Effects of a Temporary Demand Boost on Growth Rates
shaikh_observations: Output growth initially rises despite the initial fall in the profit rate because of the associated demand boost, demonstrating that demand effects can temporarily override profitability effects

The combined effects of a rise in the wage share and initial fall in unemployment serve to raise the rate of growth of productivity and of the active labor force, so that the natural rate of growth rises also
The mutual adjustment between output growth and productivity growth produces a positive association between them which has come to be called Verdoon's Law
context_paragraphs: Figure 14.4 shows that in this same case output growth initially rises despite the initial fall in the profit rate because of the associated demand boost. We also see that the combined effects of a rise in the wage share and initial fall in unemployment will serve to raise the rate of growth of productivity and of the active labor force, so that the natural rate of growth rises also.

In the end, output, growth, and the natural growth rate stay in rough balance with each other—that is, unemployment returns to a stable level. The mutual adjustment between output growth and productivity growth produces a positive association between them which has come to be called Verdoon's Law.
The important point here is that bursts in aggregate demand alter the levels of output and employment even if the growth rates of these variables are only temporarily affected.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure demonstrates the endogeneity of the natural rate of growth in classical theory, contrasting sharply with Harrodian and neoclassical approaches where it is exogenous. The mutual adjustment of output growth and productivity growth provides the micro-foundation for Verdoon's Law, showing it as an emergent property of capitalist dynamics rather than an ad hoc empirical regularity.
key_features: Output growth rises from ~2.5% to peak around ~2.75% then returns to ~2.5%
Natural rate of growth closely tracks output growth, rising from ~2.5% to ~2.75%
Productivity growth rises from ~1.7% to ~2.0% then returns to ~1.7%
All growth rates show coordinated response to demand shock
Demonstrates endogeneity of natural rate of growth
figures.appendix_reference: Appendix 14.1 for equations governing productivity and labor force responses

figures.section: III. Dynamical Interactions Between the Wage Share, Unemployment Rate, and the Harrodian 'Natural Rate' of Growth
figures.subsection: 5. Effects of productivity and labor force growth
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.5 Effects of a Temporary Demand Boost on Levels of Output, Employment, Real Wages, and Productivity Relative to Baseline Values
shaikh_observations: While the temporary demand boost effects only a temporary increase in the growth rates of output, real wages, and productivity, it causes a permanent rise in the levels of the paths of these variables relative to the baseline of no shock having taken place

The permanent level effects occur even though growth rates and unemployment return to normal, demonstrating that Keynesian-style interventions can have lasting benefits without requiring permanent stimulus
All level variables show sustained upward displacement from baseline path, with output rising most (to ~1.17), followed by real wages (~1.11), employment (~1.09), and productivity (~1.08)
context_paragraphs: Finally, figure 14.5 shows that while the temporary demand boost effects only a temporary increase in the growth rates of output, real wages, and productivity, it causes a permanent rise in the levels of the paths of these variables relative to the baseline of no shock having taken place.

The important point here is that bursts in aggregate demand alter the levels of output and employment even if the growth rates of these variables are only temporarily affected. Third, over the longer run the actual rate capacity utilization fluctuates around the normal rate through mutual adjustments in capacity and demand.
Even a temporary acceleration in demand can permanently raise the levels of the output, employment, and real wage time paths. This is of no small moment to any government.
figures.theoretical_significance: This is perhaps the most important policy implication of the classical model: temporary Keynesian interventions can produce permanent improvements in living standards even though they cannot permanently eliminate unemployment. This reconciles classical and Keynesian insights while avoiding the naive Keynesian belief that unemployment can be eliminated through demand management alone.
key_features: All variables start at baseline 1.0 and rise to permanently higher levels
Normal capacity output rises most dramatically to ~1.17 (17% above baseline)
Real wages reach ~1.11 (11% above baseline) and stabilize
Employment reaches ~1.09 (9% above baseline)
Productivity rises to ~1.08 (8% above baseline)
All show permanent level shifts despite temporary growth rate changes
related_equations: Output growth equation (14.10) integrated over time for level effects

figures.section: III. Dynamical Interactions Between the Wage Share, Unemployment Rate, and the Harrodian 'Natural Rate' of Growth
figures.subsection: 5. Effects of productivity and labor force growth
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.6 Effects of a Sustained Demand Boost on Wage Share, Unemployment, and Normal Capacity Profit Rate

axis_specifications.y_axis_right: Profit Rate and Unemployment Rate (6% to 14%)

shaikh_observations: With a sustained increase in exogenous demand, the wage share remains at a sustained higher level and the profit rate falls correspondingly, validating post-Keynesian claims about demand-led growth but with the classical caveat that unemployment still returns to normal
The unemployment rate falls for a longer time than in the temporary case but still returns to its normal level per the Classical curve, demonstrating the persistence of the Reserve Army mechanism

The sustained stimulus demonstrates that while Keynesian policy can raise wage shares and growth rates, it cannot eliminate the structural unemployment inherent to capitalism without changing power relations
context_paragraphs: The patterns in the case of a sustained increase in exogenous demand are quite different. Now we see in figure 14.6 that the wage share remains at a sustained higher level and the profit rate falls correspondingly. The unemployment rate falls for a longer time than in the previous case, but still returns to its normal level per the Classical curve summarized in equation (14.7).

The rise in the wage share and in the rate of growth of employment could be viewed as genuine successes for Keynesian fiscal policy, while the fall in the rate of profit could be viewed as the real basis of the hostility of the business class.

It should be said that the unilateral impact of the factor ε in equation 14.10 is exaggerated by the fact that its influence on the rate of growth is treated as being unmediated by profitability. Additional demand is only effective if it promises with additional profit, for otherwise there would be no incentive to expand production.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure demonstrates the limits of Keynesian demand management: while sustained stimulus can raise wage shares and benefit workers, it cannot permanently eliminate unemployment. The stubborn reversion to normal unemployment would be a puzzle for Keynesians and seems to support Friedman-Phelps NAIRU - except here the normal rate derives from competition itself, not from its absence.
key_features: Wage share rises from ~60% to ~62% and stays elevated
Profit rate falls from ~12% to ~11% and stays depressed
Unemployment rate falls initially but gradually returns toward ~10%
Unlike temporary boost, wage share and profit rate show persistent shifts
Demonstrates sustained demand effects on distribution but not on unemployment
figures.appendix_reference: Appendix 14.1 discusses limits to sustained stimulus through profitability channel
related_equations: gYR = fYR[(1 - σW) · Rn - in] + ε (14.10) with sustained positive ε

figures.section: III. Dynamical Interactions Between the Wage Share, Unemployment Rate, and the Harrodian 'Natural Rate' of Growth
figures.subsection: 5. Effects of productivity and labor force growth
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.7 Effects of a Sustained Demand Boost on Growth Rates
shaikh_observations: With sustained stimulus, the growth rates of output, productivity, and the labor force (the natural growth being the sum of the latter two) all rise to new levels, demonstrating that demand can influence long-term growth rates contrary to pure supply-side theories
Once again, we find the correlation associated with Verdoon's Law emerging from the mutual adjustment of output and productivity growth

The endogeneity of the natural rate frees accumulation from the limits of the available labor supply, though unemployment continues to influence growth through its effects on the wage share and profit rate
context_paragraphs: Figure 14.7 indicates that the growth rates of output, productivity, and the labor force (the natural growth being the sum of the latter two) all rise to new levels. Once again, we find the correlation associated with Verdoon's Law.

The endogeneity of productivity and labor force growth frees accumulation from the limits of the available labor supply, though, of course, the unemployment rate continues to influence growth through its effects on the wage share and profit rate.

The distinction between 'temporary' and 'sustained' boosts to aggregate demand is hard to pin down in real time. The essential point is that the growth rate of output gYR = fK(rn - in) + ε can be stimulated by a variety of factors that cause the expected net profit rate to differ from the normal capacity net rate.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure shows that Keynesian stimulus can have permanent effects on growth rates when sustained, not just temporary effects. This validates post-Keynesian claims about demand-led growth while maintaining the classical emphasis on profitability. The key insight is that the natural rate itself is endogenous, adapting to sustained demand changes through productivity and labor force responses.
key_features: Output growth rises from ~2.5% to sustained level around ~2.75%
Natural rate of growth rises in parallel from ~2.5% to ~2.75%
Productivity growth rises from ~1.7% to sustained level around ~1.9%
All growth rates show permanent upward shift, unlike temporary case
Verdoon's Law correlation clearly visible in parallel movements
figures.appendix_reference: Appendix 14.1 for endogenous natural rate specification

figures.subsection: 5. Effects of productivity and labor force growth
figures.full_caption: Figure 14.8 Effects of a Sustained Demand Boost on Levels of Output, Employment, Real Wages, and Productivity Relative to Baseline Values
shaikh_observations: The most striking effect is that both the level and the growth rate of real wages and productivity rise as a consequence of the sustained increase in excess demand fueled by new purchasing power
The levels of output, real wages, productivity, and employment continue to rise for some time above the paths they would have otherwise followed, with output showing the most dramatic increase

This demonstrates that sustained stimulus can produce cumulative benefits for workers through both higher wage shares and faster productivity growth, though at the cost of lower profitability which may eventually undermine the stimulus
context_paragraphs: The most striking effect shows up in figure 14.8, where now both the level and the growth rate of real wages and productivity rise as a consequence of the sustained increase in excess demand fueled by new purchasing power.

Hence, the levels of output, real wages, productivity, and employment will continue to rise for some prolonged period above the paths they would have otherwise followed. The wage share will rise and the normal rate of profit will fall correspondingly.
It is perfectly possible, therefore, that at some point, a continued stimulus would lead to a fall in the overall rate of growth [due to falling profitability undermining investment incentives].
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure reveals the most dramatic difference between temporary and sustained stimulus: sustained demand growth produces accelerating improvements in living standards through both higher levels and higher growth rates. This is the real basis of both Keynesian enthusiasm for demand management and capitalist class hostility to it, as the rising wage share threatens profitability.
key_features: Normal capacity output rises dramatically to ~1.40 (40% above baseline) and continues rising
Real wages reach ~1.22 (22% above baseline) and continue growing
Productivity rises to ~1.18 (18% above baseline) with sustained growth
Employment rises to ~1.10 (10% above baseline)
All variables show both level shifts AND increasing growth rates (upward curving paths)
Cumulative divergence from baseline much larger than temporary case

figures.full_caption: Figure 14.9 Theoretical Path of Wage Share versus Unemployment

axis_specifications.x_axis: Wage Share HP (WAGESHHP40) ranging from 0.592 to 0.632
axis_specifications.y_axis: Unemployment Rate HP (ranges visible from 0.09 to 0.114)
axis_specifications.perspective: 3D spiral visualization with time as vertical dimension

shaikh_observations: The theoretical path is an upward spiral with a clockwise movement in x, y, t space, demonstrating the predator-prey dynamics between the wage share and unemployment over time
This three-dimensional portrait shows the typical theoretical path in the face of the temporary demand boost with noise added, mimicking actual business cycle patterns

The spiral pattern reflects the classical feedback mechanism: rising wage share → falling profit rate → rising unemployment → falling wage share → rising profit rate → falling unemployment, creating persistent cycles around equilibrium
context_paragraphs: Here, it is useful to look at a three-dimensional portrait of the typical theoretical path of the wage share and the employment rate over time (the vertical axis) in the face of the temporary demand boost whose effects were previously depicted in figures 14.3–14.5 and are shown here with some noise added.

The resultant path is an upward spiral with a clockwise movement in x, y, t space where the x-axis = the wage share, the y-axis = the unemployment rate, and t = time (Flaschel 2010, 435, 448). Actual data will be examined in the next section.

Fine-grained simulation data where noise was added to equations (14.7) and (14.9) was treated as 'weekly' and converted to an annual equivalent, which was in turn lightly filtered (parameter = 3) by the Hodrick–Prescott filter. This conversion procedure aims to mimic that used in actual quarterly data shown in the empirical section of this chapter.
figures.theoretical_significance: This 3D visualization elegantly captures the essential dynamics of the classical model: the wage share and unemployment are locked in a persistent cyclical dance, spiraling around an equilibrium point but never settling down to it due to ongoing shocks. This is the formal graphical representation of Marx's Reserve Army of Labor theory, showing involuntary unemployment as an endogenous, persistent feature of capitalism.
key_features: Clockwise spiral pattern when viewed from above
Upward movement in time dimension from 1950 to 2010
Wage share oscillates roughly between 0.59 and 0.63
Unemployment oscillates roughly between 0.09 and 0.11
Multiple complete cycles visible over 60-year period
Spiral maintains rough stability around center point despite shocks
