figures.section: VI. Supply-Response
figures.full_caption: Growth Rates of Real Output, US Major Industries, 1987-2010
variables: Finance, insurance, real estate, rental, and leasing
Educational services, health care, and social assistance
Arts, entertainment, recreation, accommodation, and food services
shaikh_observations: Individual industries have stocks of inventories and imports to draw upon and can increase intensity of working day and number of shifts, allowing them to grow at different rates according to demand in the short run

As inventories dwindle and import supply stagnates, industries fall back toward their 'intrinsic' growth rates determined by input-output limits, showing that no major industry can wander off on its own for long

The figure demonstrates the interdependence of sectoral growth rates and their tendency to cluster around the economy-wide average, with visible coordinated fluctuations during recessions (2001, 2008-2009)
context_paragraphs: Actual growth is, of course, never balanced. In the short run, individual industries have stocks of inventories and imports to draw upon and can increase the intensity of the working day and the number of shifts. This allows them to grow at different rates according to demand for their products. However, as inventories dwindle and import supply stagnates, industries fall back toward their 'intrinsic' growth rates (i.e., their growth rates determined by input-output limits). Imports ease local input pressures but, of course, increase them abroad. Over the longer run, technological change can reduce the use of some inputs, but it can increase the use of others. Since the growth of any given industry requires concomitant growth in its inputs, no major industry can wander off on its own for long. This is evident in figure 15.2, which displays the growth rates of real output for major US sectors from 1987 to 2010 in comparison the economy-wide average rate (in bold).
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure provides empirical evidence for the classical argument that sectoral growth rates are constrained by input-output relationships and cannot deviate persistently from the economy-wide average. It supports the theoretical claim that the maximum rate of growth is determined by the profit rate and inter-sectoral linkages, not by labor supply constraints alone. The clustering of growth rates demonstrates the binding nature of these structural constraints on unbalanced actual growth.
key_features: Two-panel layout separating different industry groups for clarity
Bold line representing aggregate 'All industries' growth rate serves as reference point
Visible recession periods (2001, 2008-2009) show synchronized downturns across sectors
Mining and Retail trade show particularly volatile patterns in Panel A
Most sectors track relatively close to the aggregate trend despite short-term deviations
2009 recession shows the most dramatic synchronized collapse across virtually all sectors
figures.appendix_reference: None mentioned in the text. Data appears to be from standard US national accounts sources.

figures.section: VIII. Empirical Evidence - 1. United States
figures.full_caption: Growth of Nominal GDP and Relative New Purchasing Power, 1950–2010

figures.section: VIII. Empirical Evidence - 1. United States - i. Growth in nominal GDP as a function of relative new purchasing power
figures.full_caption: Growth of Nominal GDP versus Relative New Purchasing Power
variables: Relative new purchasing power (PP/Y-1, independent variable)
shaikh_observations: There is a strong positive correlation between nominal GDP growth and new purchasing power relative to GDP, confirming that the growth rate of nominal output is a function of relative new purchasing power
The Korean War years (1951-1952) appear as clear outliers with exceptionally high values for both variables, reflecting the extraordinary wartime creation of purchasing power
The scatter shows a roughly linear relationship with relatively tight clustering around the trend, supporting the hypothesis gY = f(pp) in equation (15.5)
context_paragraphs: The first hypothesis in the classical theory of inflation is that the growth of nominal output is a function of new purchasing power relative to GDP (equation (15.5)). This is entirely consistent with the notion that the portion of new purchasing power created by private banks is endogenous in the sense that it responds to the demand for private credit. We are concerned here with the impact of the sum of the private and public creation of new purchasing power, and the latter of course need not be endogenous in this sense. Figures 15.3 and 15.4 show that there is indeed a strong correlation between the nominal GDP growth and new purchasing power (1951-1952 is the Korean War), and table 15.1 confirms a robust econometric relation between the two with dummy variables for the Korean War, the Volcker Shock (see chapter 16, section II.5, and figure 16.6) and the Global Crisis, respectively.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure provides crucial empirical support for the first building block of the classical inflation theory - that nominal output growth is driven by the injection of new purchasing power into the economy. This relationship holds whether the new purchasing power comes from private bank credit creation or public (government) deficit spending. The strong correlation validates the theoretical proposition that under fiat money, virtually unlimited growth in aggregate demand can be fueled by new purchasing power, making the supply-side response the key determinant of whether this translates into real growth or inflation.
key_features: Clear positive linear relationship between the two variables
Korean War outliers (1951, 1952) explicitly labeled, showing extreme values
Tight clustering of observations around implicit trend line
Range of relative new purchasing power extends from negative (fiscal/credit contraction) to over 15% (wartime expansion)
Scatter pattern supports linear functional form assumed in subsequent econometric analysis (Table 15.1)
figures.appendix_reference: Appendices 15.1 and 15.2 provide sources, methods, and data tables for the purchasing power and GDP series.

figures.section: VIII. Empirical Evidence - 1. United States - ii. Real output growth, profitability, purchasing power, and growth utilization
figures.full_caption: Real Output Growth versus the Real Net Rate of Return on New Capital

x_axis.variable: Net real corporate incremental rate of profit

variables: Net real incremental rate of profit (rr'I = rrI - i, independent variable)
shaikh_observations: There is a strong positive correlation (0.65) between the growth rate of real output and the net real incremental rate of return, confirming that profitability is a key driver of real output growth
The 2009 Global Crisis appears as an extreme negative outlier with sharply negative profitability and real output contraction, representing the most severe disruption in the postwar period

The scatter demonstrates that net profitability (the difference between the profit rate and interest rate) motivates investment and thereby regulates real output growth, supporting the theoretical argument in equation (15.7)
context_paragraphs: The second key hypothesis of the classical theory inflation (or deflation) is that the rate growth of real output responds to purchasing power, net profitability, and the growth-utilization rate (equation (15.7)). The appropriate measure of net profitability is the real net rate of return on new investment as proxied by the net real incremental rate of profit. Figure 15.5 shows that there is a strong positive correlation (0.65) between the growth rate of real output and the net real incremental rate of return driven in good part by the correlation shown in figure 15.6 between changes in real output ΔYR and changes in ΔEBITGR (0.46). The latter correlation is expected because as the collectivity of individual firms respond to an increase in the net rate of profit this will raise aggregate real output and real profits simultaneously.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure provides empirical validation for the supply-side component of the classical inflation theory - that real output growth is motivated by net profitability. The net rate of profit (profit rate minus interest rate) represents the incentive for investment, which in turn drives capital accumulation and output expansion. This relationship is central to distinguishing the classical approach from both Keynesian demand-driven and monetarist money-supply-driven theories of inflation. When new purchasing power is created but net profitability is high, it translates into real growth rather than inflation; conversely, when profitability is low, the same purchasing power injection is more likely to cause inflation.
key_features: Strong positive correlation visible in scatter pattern
2009 labeled as extreme outlier in lower left quadrant
Net profitability measure (rrI - i) on x-axis correctly captures the incentive for investment
Wide range of profitability values (-30% to +50%) reflecting business cycle variation
Most observations cluster in positive quadrant showing typical positive profitability and growth
Correlation coefficient of 0.65 indicates robust but not deterministic relationship
figures.appendix_reference: Appendices 15.1 and 15.2 provide sources and methods for profit and output data. Equation (15.2) defines the net real incremental rate of profit.

figures.section: VIII. Empirical Evidence - 1. United States - ii. Real output growth, profitability, purchasing power, and growth utilization
figures.full_caption: Change in Real Output versus Change in Real Gross Profits
variables: Change in real output (ΔYR, dependent variable)
Change in real gross profits (ΔEBITGR, independent variable)
shaikh_observations: There is a positive correlation (0.46) between changes in real output and changes in real gross profits, demonstrating that as firms collectively respond to changes in profitability, both aggregate output and aggregate profits move together
The 2008-2009 crisis period shows extreme negative values for both variables, with 2009 representing the most severe simultaneous contraction in output and profits in the dataset

The simultaneity of output and profit changes reflects the microeconomic reality that individual firms' investment decisions in response to profitability have macroeconomic consequences for aggregate demand, output, and realized profits
context_paragraphs: Figure 15.5 shows that there is a strong positive correlation (0.65) between the growth rate of real output and the net real incremental rate of return driven in good part by the correlation shown in figure 15.6 between changes in real output ΔYR and changes in ΔEBITGR (0.46). The latter correlation is expected because as the collectivity of individual firms respond to an increase in the net rate of profit this will raise aggregate real output and real profits simultaneously.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure addresses a potential endogeneity concern in the relationship between output and profitability shown in Figure 15.5. Since output growth affects realized profits, there is a simultaneous relationship. The correlation between changes (first differences) in output and profits demonstrates that the positive association is not spurious but reflects the fundamental relationship whereby profitability motivates investment, which drives output, which in turn generates profits. This validates the use of profitability as an explanatory variable for real output growth in the inflation model.
key_features: First-difference specification (changes rather than levels) reduces autocorrelation
Positive correlation visible despite considerable scatter
2008 and 2009 labeled as extreme crisis observations
2009 shows the largest negative change in both variables
Origin-centered scatter pattern reflects both expansions and contractions
Correlation of 0.46 is moderate, reflecting the complex simultaneous relationship
figures.appendix_reference: Appendices 15.1 and 15.2 provide sources and methods. EBITGR refers to Earnings Before Interest and Taxes in real (inflation-adjusted) terms, approximating gross profits.

figures.section: VIII. Empirical Evidence - 1. United States - iii. Inflation in the United States
figures.full_caption: Normalized Inflation and Growth Utilization Rates
variables: Inflation rate (normalized: deviation from mean divided by standard deviation)
Growth utilization rate σ' = I/P (normalized: deviation from mean divided by standard deviation)
shaikh_observations: The normalized growth utilization rate and normalized inflation rate track each other reasonably well over the entire period, showing positive co-movement during the 1970s inflation surge and the subsequent decline

The post-1982 neoliberal era shows both series shifted to lower normalized values compared to the pre-1981 period, indicating that a given level of growth utilization elicits lower inflation in the later period

The divergences between the two series reflect the influence of other factors in the inflation equation (new purchasing power and net profitability), validating the multi-factor classical model rather than a simple mechanical relationship
context_paragraphs: An interesting feature of the classical charts is that all the points in the pre-neoliberal era from 1951 to 1981 are entirely above the 45-degree line; whereas, most of the ones in the post-neoliberal era are below that reference line. In effect, a given level of unutilized growth potential elicits a lower rate of inflation in the second period. This same drop-off can be seen from a different perspective in figure 15.10 in which we see that the later era has relatively lower normalized inflation rates in comparison to the corresponding normalized growth-utilization rates.
figures.theoretical_significance: This normalization technique allows for direct visual comparison of two variables that have different scales and distributions. The figure demonstrates that the relationship between growth utilization and inflation is not constant over time - the neoliberal era (post-1982) shows a structural break with lower inflation for given levels of growth utilization. This shift is consistent with the weakening of labor bargaining power and welfare state retrenchment documented in Chapter 14. The figure supports the theoretical argument that the classical inflation relationship π = F(pp, rr'I) + β·(1-σ') should include shift factors that capture institutional and policy regime changes.
key_features: Dual time series plot with normalized variables
Solid and dashed lines represent inflation and growth utilization respectively
Clear structural break visible around 1982 (Reagan era)
1970s period shows both variables elevated (normalized values above 1)
Post-1982 period shows both variables at lower normalized levels
Normalization allows comparison despite different underlying units and scales
Visible tracking between the two series with some divergences
figures.appendix_reference: Appendices 15.1 and 15.2 provide underlying data. Growth utilization rate σ' defined as I/P in equation discussion.

figures.section: VIII. Empirical Evidence - 1. United States - iii. Inflation in the United States
figures.full_caption: HP(100) Trend of the Net Incremental Rate of Profit

y_axis.variable: Net real incremental rate of profit (HP trend)

variables: HP(100) filtered trend of net real incremental rate of profit (rr'I = rrI - i)
Smoothing parameter λ = 100 for annual data
shaikh_observations: The net rate of profit shows a long-term declining trend from approximately 15% in the early 1950s to near-zero or negative levels by the late 1970s, consistent with the falling rate of profit documented elsewhere in the book
Beginning in the early 1980s (Reagan/neoliberal era), the trend reverses and rises substantially, reaching levels of 5-7% by the mid-2000s before the financial crisis

The trend pattern helps explain the shift in the inflation relationship between the pre-1981 and post-1981 periods: the falling profit rate in the first period would have contributed to higher inflation via reduced real output growth, while the rising rate in the second period would have contributed to lower inflation
context_paragraphs: From 1948 to 1981, relative new purchasing power rises and then stabilizes (figure 15.3) while the HP(100) trend of the net real incremental rate of profit falls in that same period and then rises thereafter (figure 15.11). This implies that in the first period the two effects would make a net positive contribution to the shift term F(pp, rr'I). On the other hand, in the second period, pp is falling and rr'I is generally rising. This implies a net negative contribution to the intercept term, other things being equal. Hence, on these grounds alone we would expect the points in the latter period to be shifted down in the second period—just as we find in figures 15.8 and 15.9.
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure is crucial for understanding the structural shift in the inflation relationship between the pre- and post-neoliberal eras. The HP filter isolates the long-term trend from cyclical fluctuations, revealing the fundamental movement in profitability. The declining trend through 1980 represents the crisis of profitability that motivated the neoliberal turn, while the subsequent recovery reflects the success of neoliberal policies in restoring profit rates through wage suppression, deregulation, and globalization. In the inflation equation π = F(pp, rr'I, σ'), this trend in rr'I acts as a shift factor that helps explain why the same growth-utilization rate produced different inflation outcomes in different eras.
key_features: Smooth trend line using Hodrick-Prescott filter with λ=100
Clear U-shaped pattern with trough around 1980
Peak in early 1950s around 15%
Minimum in late 1970s/early 1980s near zero or negative
Recovery to 5-10% range by 2000s
Sharp spike at end (2008) likely reflects crisis-related data volatility
HP filtering removes business cycle fluctuations to reveal underlying trend
figures.appendix_reference: Appendices 15.1 and 15.2 provide sources and methods for profit rate data. Equation (15.2) defines net real incremental rate of profit as rr'It ≈ ΔPGRt - i·IGRt-1 / IGRt-1 = rrIt - i

figures.section: II. PROFITABILITY IN THE POSTWAR PERIOD - 1. Normal and actual profit rates
figures.full_caption: Actual and Normal Profit Rates and Profit Shares

y_axis.note: All data is on a log scale so that the slope of each curve represents its growth rate

variables: Rn (normal maximum profit rate / capacity-capital ratio)
shaikh_observations: The normal maximum profit rate (Rn) falls steadily throughout the postwar period, indicating that technical change is consistently capital-biased

The normal profit share is essentially stable in US labor's 'golden age' from 1947-1968, falls during the Stagflation Crisis of 1969-1982, then rises considerably during the neoliberal era starting in the 1980s

The normal profit rate falls during the golden age, falls faster during the Stagflation Crisis, then begins to flatten out during the neoliberal era right through the current crisis due to rising normal profit share counteracting the fall in the normal maximum profit rate
context_paragraphs: The actual profit rate can be decomposed into structural and cyclical factors as the product of the profit share and the output-capital ratio: r = P/K = (P/Y) · (Y/Yn) · (Yn/K) = σ · Rn · uK

Since uK = 1 when output is at normal capacity, the normal profit rate can be written as the product of the normal profit share σn calculated as its HP-filtered trend, and the capacity-capital ratio: rn = σn · Rn
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure demonstrates the decomposition of profit rates into structural (capacity-capital ratio, profit share) and cyclical (capacity utilization) components. It shows how technical change consistently erodes the maximum profit rate, while the profit share becomes the key variable that can counteract this tendency. The neoliberal shift in the 1980s fundamentally altered the trajectory of profitability by attacking labor and raising the profit share.
key_features: Logarithmic scale allows comparison of growth rates across different variables
Clear periodization: Golden Age (1947-1968), Stagflation Crisis (1969-1982), Boom (1982-2007), Global Crisis (2007+)
Distinction between actual and normal (trend) values for all three measures
Visual evidence of capital-biased technical change throughout entire period
Structural break in profit share corresponding to neoliberal attack on labor in 1980s
figures.appendix_reference: Data tables in Appendix 16.2; measurement methodology from Chapter 6 and Appendices 6.1-6.8

figures.section: II. PROFITABILITY IN THE POSTWAR PERIOD - 3. Impact on profitability of the suppression of real wage growth
figures.full_caption: Actual and Counterfactual Rates of Profit of US Corporations, 1947-2011
variables: Counterfactual Path of the Corporate Profit Rate (if real wages had maintained their postwar relation to productivity)
shaikh_observations: The repression directed against labor beginning in the Reagan era had the clear effect of reversing the postwar pattern of profitability
Had business real wages maintained their postwar relation to business productivity, the profit rate would have continued its downward trajectory through the neoliberal era
The divergence between actual and counterfactual profit rates beginning in the 1980s demonstrates the material foundation of the neoliberal boom in the suppression of real wage growth
context_paragraphs: Real wages tend to grow more slowly than productivity even under normal circumstances, but starting in the 1980s in the Reagan era real wage growth slowed down considerably

This departure from their historical trend was brought about through a two-pronged strategy: attacks on private and public institutions that supported labor and a surge in globalization which brought the world's large pool of cheap labor into more direct competition with labor markets in the developed world
figures.theoretical_significance: This counterfactual analysis isolates the specific impact of wage suppression on profitability. It demonstrates that the reversal of falling profit rates in the neoliberal era was not due to technological improvements or enhanced productivity growth, but rather to a structural shift in the distribution of income away from labor. This is crucial evidence for understanding the class dynamics underlying the post-1980s boom.
key_features: Counterfactual methodology showing alternative historical path
Clear divergence point in early 1980s corresponding to Reagan-era policies
Shows profit rate would have fallen to approximately 2-4% by 2007 without wage suppression
Actual profit rate maintained at 6-8% level through neoliberal period
Demonstrates approximately 4 percentage point boost to profit rate from wage suppression
figures.appendix_reference: Counterfactual constructed using 1947-1981 relationship between real compensation and productivity from Figure 16.3; profit rate data from Appendix 16.2

figures.section: II. PROFITABILITY IN THE POSTWAR PERIOD - 4. Rate of return on average capital versus new investment
figures.full_caption: Corporate Average and Current (Real) Smoothed Incremental Rates of Profit
variables: rI HP100(-1) (lagged HP-smoothed corporate current real incremental rate of profit)
shaikh_observations: The average and incremental rates of profit follow similar paths: both drift downward in the immediate postwar decades, both benefit greatly from the Vietnam War/Great Society boost, both fall thereafter
Both rates increase in the neoliberal era, although the incremental rate responds more strongly to the boost provided by the dot.com bubble of the 1990s
The incremental rate shows greater volatility than the average rate, reflecting its sensitivity to current investment conditions
context_paragraphs: Figure 16.5 compares the average corporate rate of profit (which is also a real rate) with the lagged value of the HP-smoothed corporate current (real) incremental rate
Both benefit greatly from the Vietnam War/Great Society boost (see the effect on the rate of capacity utilization depicted in appendix 6.6, figure 6.6.1)
figures.theoretical_significance: This comparison between average and incremental profit rates is crucial for understanding investment dynamics. The incremental rate measures the return on new investment and is therefore more forward-looking and sensitive to current conditions. The general co-movement of the two rates validates the profit rate as a key driver of accumulation, while the greater volatility of the incremental rate explains why investment can be particularly sensitive to short-term fluctuations.
key_features: Both rates show secular decline from 1947 through early 1980s
Sharp spike in both rates during Vietnam War/Great Society period (late 1960s)
Incremental rate shows greater amplitude of fluctuations
Incremental rate shows stronger response to dot.com bubble (late 1990s)
Both rates rise in neoliberal era but remain below early postwar levels
Incremental rate lagged by one year to reflect decision-making dynamics
figures.appendix_reference: Data from Appendix 16.2; HP-smoothing parameter of 100; capacity utilization effects shown in Appendix 6.6, Figure 6.6.1

figures.section: II. PROFITABILITY IN THE POSTWAR PERIOD - 5. The extraordinary postwar path of the interest rate
figures.full_caption: US Rate of Interest, 1947-2011 (3-Month T-Bill)
shaikh_observations: In the first phase from 1947 to 1981, the interest rate rose twenty-four-fold, from 0.59% in 1947 to 14.03% in 1981. In the second phase, from 1981 onward, it fell equally dramatically, going from 14.03% to a wispy 0.06% in 2011

The co-movement until the 1980s between the price level and the interest rate (Gibson's Law) held until monetary policy severed the connection and forced the interest rate to fall beginning in the early 1980s

The 'Volcker Shock' may have slowed inflation but did not end it, and the same long postwar swings in interest rates obtained in most major capitalist countries, demonstrating that policy-induced decreases were characteristic of the whole capitalist center
context_paragraphs: The secular postwar decline in average and incremental rates of profit was reversed in the 1980s by means of an unparalleled slowdown in real wage growth. But this is only part of the explanation for the resulting great boom for at the same time there was an extraordinary fall in the interest rate that greatly increased the net rates
The 'Volcker Shock' is often credited with 'ending' inflation by sharply raising the short-term interest rate from 10% in 1979 to a peak of 14% in mid-1981 (and the prime rate from 11.2% to of 20%)
With short-term interest rates hovering around zero there is no further room to maneuver on this front. As of 2011 the US rate stood at 0.0006 (i.e., 0.06%)
figures.theoretical_significance: This figure demonstrates the breakdown of Gibson's Law (the historical co-movement of interest rates and price levels) through active monetary policy intervention. The extraordinary 24-fold rise and subsequent collapse of interest rates represents one of the most dramatic policy-driven shifts in modern capitalism. The fall in interest rates was as important as wage suppression in generating the neoliberal boom by raising the net rate of profit (r - i).
key_features: Two distinct phases: rising (1947-1981) and falling (1981-2011)
Co-movement with GDP deflator until early 1980s (Gibson's Law)
Severing of Gibson's Law relationship through monetary policy
Volcker Shock clearly visible as spike 1979-1981
Near-zero interest rates by 2011 (0.06%)
Interest rate at policy lower bound with no room for further conventional monetary policy
figures.appendix_reference: Interest rate data from Appendix 16.2; Gibson's Law theoretically derived in Chapter 11, Section 6 and displayed in Figure 11.6; comparison with OECD rates in Figure 16.7
